title: The neuroscience of why some people develop PTSD and others do not
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, looking at the floor, trying to understand why I was the one who broke. Two siblings, same childhood, sa...
metaDescription: I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, looking at the floor, trying to understand why I was the one who broke. Two siblings, same childhoo...
htmlContent:
I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, looking at the floor, trying to understand why I was the one who broke. Two siblings, same childhood, same house, same parents. One of them seemed fine. The other, me, was a wreck. My therapist leaned forward and said something I'll never forget: "The question isn't what happened to you. The question is what happened inside your nervous system." That's what we're talking about today.

Here's the thing. Two people can walk through the exact same fire. One emerges with PTSD, their nervous system locked in a permanent state of alarm. The other, somehow, keeps moving. It's not about who's stronger. It's not about who's more broken. It's about wiring. It's about biology. It's about what your nervous system learned before you could even speak.

I want to walk you through the neuroscience of why some people develop PTSD and others don't. Not from a textbook. From the ground up. From the body up. Stay with me here.

The nervous system as a prediction machine

Your brain is not a passive recorder of reality. It's a prediction machine. Every millisecond, your brain scans your environment, checks your internal state, and guesses what's coming next. This isn't philosophy. This is neuroscience. Dr. Lisa Feldman Barrett, a neuroscientist at Northeastern University, has spent decades showing that your brain constructs your experience of the world based on past learning. It doesn't just react. It anticipates.

So when you walk into a room, your brain has already made a bet about whether that room is safe or dangerous. That bet is based on your history. Your childhood. Your attachment patterns. Your previous traumas. If your brain has learned, over and over, that the world is threatening, it will predict threat everywhere. Even in silence. Even in safety.

This is where PTSD starts to make sense. It's not that something terrible happened to you. It's that your nervous system learned a lesson it can't unlearn. The lesson is: danger is coming. Always. Be ready. And that lesson gets wired into your body at a level deeper than words.

Think about it. Two people in a car accident. Same crash. Same injuries. One develops PTSD. The other doesn't. The difference isn't the event. The difference is what each person's nervous system brought into that car. One had a history of safety, of being soothed, of having their distress met with care. The other had a history of unpredictability, of being left alone in fear, of learning that no one was coming. The crash didn't create the trauma. The crash activated a nervous system that was already primed for it.

That's hard to sit with. I know. It can feel like blame. It's not. It's biology. And biology can change.

The polyvagal theory and the ladder of survival

Dr. Stephen Porges, the man who gave us polyvagal theory, changed how I understand my own body. He showed that our nervous system has a hierarchy. A ladder. At the top is social engagement, where you can connect, talk, feel safe. Below that is fight or flight, the sympathetic activation that gets you ready to run or fight. And at the bottom is shutdown, the dorsal vagal response where your body goes numb, collapses, dissociates.

Most people think of trauma as fight or flight. But the deeper wound, the one that really sets the stage for PTSD, is the inability to get back up the ladder. You get stuck. Your nervous system can't find its way back to social engagement. It keeps dropping down. Over and over. Every loud noise. Every unexpected touch. Every moment of silence that feels too heavy.

Dr. Porges calls this "neuroception." It's your nervous system's ability to detect safety and danger without your conscious mind having any say in it. You can't think your way out of a neuroception. You can't tell yourself "I'm safe" and expect your body to believe it. Your body has its own intelligence. And if that intelligence has learned that the world is dangerous, it will keep you in survival mode no matter what your thinking brain says.

Here's where it gets personal for me. For years, I couldn't understand why I'd freeze in situations that weren't actually threatening. A disagreement with a friend. A crowded grocery store. A text message that felt slightly off. My body would go cold. My voice would disappear. I'd feel like I was watching myself from a distance. That's the dorsal vagal response. That's the bottom of the ladder. And it was my home address.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. She translates Porges' work into exercises you can actually use. It's not just theory. It's a map for your nervous system.

The role of early attachment and the developing brain

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has said that the single most important factor in whether someone develops PTSD is whether they had a safe attachment figure early in life. Not the severity of the trauma. Not the type of trauma. Attachment.

Here's why. Your brain develops in the context of relationship. When you're a baby, you don't have a fully formed prefrontal cortex. You don't have the ability to calm yourself down. You rely on your caregiver to regulate your nervous system for you. When you cry, they pick you up. When you're scared, they hold you. When you're overwhelmed, they soothe you. Over time, your brain internalizes that pattern. You learn, at a body level, that distress is temporary. That help arrives. That you're not alone.

But what if that doesn't happen? What if your caregiver is unpredictable, or absent, or themselves traumatized? Then your nervous system learns a different lesson. Distress is endless. No one is coming. You are alone. And your brain develops around that core belief. It becomes hypervigilant. It scans for threat constantly. It prepares for the worst because the worst has happened before.

This is why two people can experience the same event and have completely different outcomes. One has a nervous system that knows, deep in its bones, that safety exists. That person can experience something terrible and eventually find their way back to regulation. The other has a nervous system that has never known safety. That person's body doesn't have a template for recovery. The trauma doesn't end because the body never learned how to end it.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Dr. Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That shift matters. It changes the whole conversation.

The neurobiology of resilience and the window of tolerance

Dr. Dan Siegel, a clinical professor of psychiatry at UCLA, introduced the concept of the "window of tolerance." This is the zone where your nervous system can handle life. You can think clearly. You can feel your emotions without being overwhelmed. You can connect with others. When you're inside your window, you're resilient. When you're outside it, you're either hyperaroused (anxious, angry, panicked) or hypoaroused (numb, depressed, dissociated).

Trauma shrinks your window of tolerance. It makes you more sensitive to triggers. It means smaller and smaller things can knock you out of your window. A raised voice. A sudden change in plans. A smell that reminds you of something you can't quite name. And once you're out, it's hard to get back in. Your nervous system doesn't have the brakes it needs.

Resilience, then, isn't about being tough. It's about having a wide window of tolerance. It's about being able to experience distress without losing your ability to regulate. And the size of your window is largely determined by your early life experiences. But here's the good news. It's not fixed. You can widen your window. You can teach your nervous system new patterns. It takes time. It takes patience. It takes work. But it's possible.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's full of exercises that help you expand your window of tolerance. I've used some of them myself. They're not magic. They're practice. But practice changes the brain.

What this means for recovery

I want to be honest with you. Understanding the neuroscience doesn't make the pain go away. It doesn't make the flashbacks stop. It doesn't give you a quick fix. What it does is give you a map. It helps you understand why your body does what it does. And that understanding, over time, can reduce the shame.

Shame is the voice that says "I'm broken." Shame is the belief that you should be over it by now. Shame is the feeling that you're weak for struggling. But when you understand the neuroscience, you realize something crucial. You're not broken. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it was trained to do. It's protecting you. It's just using old information. It's fighting a war that ended years ago, but your body doesn't know that yet.

Recovery isn't about erasing the past. It's about giving your nervous system new information. It's about small, repeated experiences of safety. It's about finding people who can co-regulate with you. It's about learning to stay present in your body even when it's uncomfortable. It's slow. It's nonlinear. It's frustrating. But it's real.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Dr. Gabor Maté. He shows how the stress of unprocessed trauma shows up in the body. It's a hard read. But it's an important one.

I can't tell you why you developed PTSD and someone else didn't. I don't know your story. But I can tell you that it's not your fault. It's not a character flaw. It's not a lack of willpower. It's your nervous system, doing its job, based on what it learned. And that nervous system can learn new things. It can change. It can heal.

Not completely. Not perfectly. But enough. Enough to find moments of peace. Enough to feel connection again. Enough to trust that you're not alone.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Dr. Gabor Maté connects the dots between stress and illness.
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Dr. Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey offer a compassionate reframing of trauma.
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana makes polyvagal theory practical for healing.
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana offers a practical guide to working with your nervous system.
